source.title: Corticostriatal functional connectivity predicts transition to chronic back pain

confidence_rationale: Strong longitudinal human imaging dataset directly addressing the slow-drift question SD-032e is built around. The drift localises to corticostriatal (mPFC-NAc) circuitry rather than pACC-to-interoceptive-baseline, so mapping_fidelity is moderate: the paper licenses the *general* claim that sustained nociceptive exposure reshapes a slow homeostatic/reward baseline, but does not directly evidence a pACC write into SD-012's drive_level. Transfer risk is moderate because REE's drive_level is a single scalar while the paper demonstrates distributed network-level reorganisation.
failure_signatures: If sustained z_harm_a produces no measurable drift in any slow homeostatic variable over long horizons, SD-032e is falsified in the direction this paper anticipates.

If the drift is observable but is mediated entirely by dopaminergic/NAc-analog circuitry and bypasses the interoceptive baseline, SD-032e's choice of drive_level as the write target is mislocalised — the correct target would be a reward-predictor module that REE does not currently have.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Individuals who transition from subacute to chronic back pain show progressive reorganisation of mPFC-NAc functional connectivity; this corticostriatal pattern predicts transition better than properties of the nociceptive input itself.
mapping.ree_translation: Sustained affective-pain exposure (persistent z_harm_a) drives slow reshaping of a baseline homeostatic/reward signal. In REE terms, this licenses the general SD-032e architecture: z_harm_a must be able to influence a slow-timescale variable that then gates salience and action evaluation. The paper does NOT specify the target as drive_level per se; it localises the drift in corticostriatal (reward/valuation) circuitry.
mapping.mapping_caveat: REE's drive_level is an interoceptive/energetic scalar (SD-012), not a reward-predictor. Baliki 2012 evidences a slow-plasticity reshaping in mPFC-NAc, which is architecturally closer to a value/reward baseline than to a homeostatic drive. Citing this paper as direct support for 'pACC writes into drive_level' overreaches. It supports the broader thesis that sustained nociceptive input shifts some slow baseline; it does NOT disambiguate which baseline.
mapping.source_context: Longitudinal human fMRI, n=39 subacute back pain patients, 3 scans over ~1 year; 16 healthy controls.

confidence_components.notes: High source quality (Nature Neuroscience, longitudinal design, published reanalyses). Mapping fidelity pulled down because the paper localises drift in reward circuitry, not interoceptive baseline. Transfer risk moderate: human clinical-to-agent is always non-trivial, but the architectural claim (slow drift exists) transfers cleanly; the specific target does not.
